source: Quranic Arabic Corpus — corpus.quran.com (University of Leeds)

sections.name: The Essentials
sections.description: Learn the most fundamental Quranic words and Al-Fatiha

units.name: In the Name of Allah
units.description: Learn the Bismillah and the meaning of "al-" (the)

grammarTip.title: The Definite Article ال
grammarTip.explanation: In Arabic, "ال" (al-) means "the". Adding it to a word makes it definite. For example, رَحْمَة means "mercy", but الرَّحْمَة means "THE mercy". Almost every word in the Bismillah uses this pattern.

units.name: Praise & Lordship
units.description: Learn words of praise and how Arabic shows possession

grammarTip.title: الدِّين — Religion AND Judgement
grammarTip.explanation: The root د-ي-ن has a dual meaning in the Quran. In يَوْمِ الدِّينِ (1:4), it means "the Day of Judgement". But in لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ (109:6), it means "your religion/way of life". Context determines which meaning applies — both come from the same root.

units.name: Worship & Guidance
units.description: Learn the words of worship and asking for guidance

grammarTip.title: إِيَّاكَ = "You alone" (Emphasis)
grammarTip.explanation: Normally in Arabic the verb already includes "you". But إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ puts "You" at the front for emphasis — "YOU ALONE we worship." This structure is called تَقْدِيم (fronting).

examples.english: You alone we ask for help

units.name: Al-Fatiha Mastery
units.description: Complete your understanding of the whole of Surah Al-Fatiha

units.name: The Oneness of God
units.concept: Surah Al-Ikhlas — قل هو الله أحد
units.description: Learn every word of Surah Al-Ikhlas and its deep meaning

grammarTip.title: قُلْ — "Say!" (Commands)
grammarTip.explanation: قُلْ is a command form of the verb قَالَ (to say). In the Quran, Allah commands the Prophet (ﷺ) to "Say!" — this form appears 332 times. Commands in Arabic are formed from the present-tense verb.

examples.english: Say: He is Allah, the One

units.name: Pronouns
units.description: Learn how Arabic uses pronouns — both standalone and attached to words

grammarTip.title: Attached Pronouns
grammarTip.explanation: Arabic attaches pronouns to the end of words instead of using separate words. "كِتَابُهُ" means "his book" — the "هُ" (hu) at the end means "his". This is one of Arabic's most powerful shortcuts.

units.name: Common Particles
units.description: Learn the small words that connect everything together

grammarTip.title: Particles (حُرُوف)
grammarTip.explanation: Arabic particles are small words that show relationships between other words. They don't change form and are among the most common words in the Quran. Knowing just 8 particles lets you recognise a huge portion of the Quran!

units.name: Salah — What You Say
units.description: Understand the words you say five times a day in prayer

grammarTip.title: Understanding Your Salah
grammarTip.explanation: Every phrase in your prayer has a meaning. When you say "سُبْحَانَ رَبِّيَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ" in ruku, you're saying "Glory be to my Lord, the Most Great." The Tashahud — "التَّحِيَّاتُ لِلَّهِ" — means "All greetings are for Allah." Understanding these words transforms your prayer.
grammarTip.sourceNote: Standard Islamic prayer texts. Tashahud: Sahih al-Bukhari 831 (sunnah.com/bukhari:831)

examples.english: Glory be to my Lord, the Most Great

sections.name: Building Blocks
sections.description: Expand your vocabulary with Allah's names, protection, and grammar patterns

units.name: Allah's Names
units.description: Learn the Beautiful Names of Allah and the adjective pattern they follow

grammarTip.title: The فَعِيل Pattern
grammarTip.explanation: Many of Allah's names follow the فَعِيل (fa'eel) pattern — a common Arabic pattern for intensified adjectives. عَلِيم (All-Knowing), سَمِيع (All-Hearing), قَدِير (All-Powerful) all follow this pattern from their root letters.

units.name: Seeking Protection
units.description: Learn the vocabulary of Surah Al-Falaq and the concept of seeking protection

grammarTip.title: أَعُوذُ بِاللَّهِ — Seeking Refuge
grammarTip.explanation: أَعُوذُ means "I seek refuge" — it's a verb in the present tense with "I" built into it. بِ means "in/with". Together, أَعُوذُ بِاللَّهِ means "I seek refuge in Allah". This phrase is used before reciting the Quran.

examples.english: I seek refuge in the Lord of daybreak
I seek refuge in the Lord of mankind

units.name: Protection from Evil
units.concept: Surah An-Nas — refuge from the whisperer
units.description: Learn every word of Surah An-Nas and the concept of seeking protection from evil whispers

grammarTip.title: The Three-fold Attribution
grammarTip.explanation: Surah An-Nas begins with three attributes of Allah — رَبِّ ٱلنَّاسِ (Lord of mankind), مَلِكِ ٱلنَّاسِ (King of mankind), إِلَـٰهِ ٱلنَّاسِ (God of mankind). The progression from Rabb (Nurturer) to Malik (Sovereign) to Ilaah (the One worshipped) represents ascending levels of authority.
grammarTip.sourceNote: Classical tafsir convention (Ibn Kathir, public domain)

units.name: Ayat al-Kursi
units.description: Learn the key vocabulary of the greatest ayah in the Quran

grammarTip.title: لَا إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — Negation for Emphasis
grammarTip.explanation: Ayat al-Kursi opens with لَا إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "There is no deity except Him." This uses لَا to negate + إِلَّا to except, creating the strongest possible affirmation of monotheism. This is a nominal sentence (جُمْلَة اسْمِيَّة) — it has no verb, expressing a permanent, unchanging truth.
grammarTip.sourceNote: Sahih Muslim 810: "The greatest ayah in the Book of Allah is Ayat al-Kursi." Standard Arabic grammar (nahw)

examples.english: There is no deity except Him
There is no deity except Allah

vocabulary.translation: There is no deity except Him

units.name: Good & Evil
units.description: Learn how the Quran talks about good and evil deeds, even the smallest

grammarTip.title: Opposites: خَيْر (good) vs شَرّ (evil)
grammarTip.explanation: The Quran often pairs opposites for emphasis. فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُ — "Whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it." The same structure is repeated with شَرّ (evil). This parallelism is a key Quranic literary device.

units.name: Time & Loss
units.description: Learn how Arabic emphasises with إِنَّ and the message of time

grammarTip.title: إِنَّ — "Indeed" (Emphasis)
grammarTip.explanation: إِنَّ is an emphatic particle meaning "indeed" or "surely". It emphasises whatever comes after it. In Surah Al-Asr: إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَفِى خُسْرٍ — "Indeed, mankind is in loss." The لَ before فِى doubles the emphasis.

units.name: Belief & Action
units.description: Learn words about belief, righteous action, truth, and patience

grammarTip.title: Root ع-م-ل: Work & Deeds
grammarTip.explanation: The root ع-م-ل gives us عَمَل (deed/work), عَمِلُوا (they did), and عَامِل (worker). In the Quran, faith (إِيمَان) is almost always paired with righteous deeds (عَمِلُوا ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ) — belief without action isn't enough.

units.name: Creation & Nature
units.description: Learn the first words ever revealed and vocabulary about creation

grammarTip.title: Root خ-ل-ق: To Create
grammarTip.explanation: The root خ-ل-ق gives us خَلَقَ (He created), خَلْق (creation), and خَالِق (Creator). The very first revealed ayah was اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ — "Read in the name of your Lord who created."

units.name: Negation
units.description: Learn how Arabic says "no" in five different ways

grammarTip.title: Five Ways to Say "Not"
grammarTip.explanation:
Arabic has 5 negation words, each for a different situation:
• لَا — general "no/not"
• مَا — negates past or present
• لَمْ — "did not" (negates past)
• لَنْ — "will not" (negates future)
• لَيْسَ — "is not"

units.name: Short Surah Mastery
units.concept: Review Al-Ikhlas, Al-Falaq, An-Nas, Al-Asr & Ayat al-Kursi
units.description: Review and master vocabulary from all the surahs and ayahs you've learned
